theme: Review #102 — The dashboard watches the team, but the team should feel watched back. This review is about making every status change a moment, not a state flip.

ideas.title: Monitor Boot Sequence on Status Wake-Up
ideas.description: When a worker transitions from idle (screensaver) to working, the monitor switches instantly — no drama. Add a 400ms boot animation: a quick flash, a cursor blink, then a progress bar before the working SVG loads in. DESK_SCREENSAVER_SVG → DESK_SCREEN_SVG already swaps on status change, so the hook is right there. The boot moment gives viewers a satisfying 'they're back' beat and makes status changes feel physical, not boolean.

ideas.title: Error Postmortem Card on Recovery
ideas.description: When a worker flips from error → working, nothing marks the moment except a status label change. Float a tiny 'incident resolved' card up from their desk with a timestamp and a one-liner in their voice — Carlos: 'root cause: the logs lied', Dave: 'pipeline green. do not ask what I did', Sofia: 'test added. you are welcome.' The PING_REACTIONS already establish each worker's voice. Turning error recovery into a narrative closure makes the on-fire → flow transition emotionally satisfying instead of invisible.

ideas.title: After-Hours Office Deepens into Night
ideas.description: The WindowElement already swaps to a night-sky gradient at after-hours, but the office floor looks identical day and night. When vibe hits after-hours, shift the whole interior: darken floor tiles, warm desk lamps to amber, turn ambient-particle color to moonlight-blue, add faint star-dots to the ceiling area via a CSS layer. The data-vibe attribute is already on .office-floor — it's a pure CSS addition. The office should LOOK like night, not just be labeled it.

ideas.title: Animated Vacancy Job Board on Empty Desks
ideas.description: VACANT_ADS already define a title + req per vibe, but they render as static text on the monitor. Make the empty desk monitor cycle through animated slides: a scrolling marquee for slow-day, urgent red blinker for on-fire, a minimal centered haiku for after-hours. The DESK_SCREEN_SVG infrastructure already exists per worker — a 'vacant' SVG variant driven by vibeKey would drop right in. Empty desks are real estate; they should tell a story instead of sitting blank.

ideas.title: Co-Ship Spark Line Between Desks
ideas.description: When two workers complete tasks within 60 seconds of each other, briefly draw a glowing animated line between their desk positions (using DESKS coordinate data already in constants.js). Color it per the slower worker's role color from ROLE_COLORS. No new data needed — just watch the activityEntries stream for back-to-back 'complete' events. Turns invisible collaboration into a visible spatial moment. The office floor becomes a social graph, if only for a second.

ideas.title: Bloberto Reacts to Task Completions
ideas.description: VIBE_QUOTES exists and fires on a 45-second ambient loop, but Bloberto never reacts to real events. When a worker completes a task, trigger a 3-second speech bubble on Bloberto with a vibe-matched quote from the existing pool — plus add a small set of completion-specific lines ('That one's going in the retro as a win.', 'Per my roadmap, this was expected.'). Right now Bloberto is passive furniture. He should feel like a manager who notices things, even if he takes credit for them.

ideas.title: Luna's Ideas Panel in the Header
ideas.description: ideas.json is already fetched (fetchIdeasFromGitHub is wired up) and used only for the review milestone confetti. Surface the review theme and top 2-3 ideas in an expandable tooltip or slide-in panel triggered by a 🌙 badge in the header when a fresh review drops. Users watching the dashboard would see the creative direction of the product they're watching. Meta, yes. But it closes a beautiful loop: the dashboard narrates its own evolution in real time.

ideas.title: Lone Survivor Shadow Mode
ideas.description: LONE_SURVIVOR_LABELS already write evocative flavor text when one worker remains (e.g. '🌙 last one standing'). But the character looks exactly the same as when the office is full. When a worker is the lone active presence, render them with a longer CSS drop-shadow, a slightly dimmer ambient particle field, and — if after-hours — a faint echo of their character silhouette offset by 2px behind them. The drama the copy promises should exist in the visual. Right now the text outperforms the scene.
